coredata.dc:title: Design and optimization of a Cr(VI)-Selective Electrode based on a polymeric ß-cyclodextrin membrane modified with sulfur donor groups
coredata.prism:copyright: © 2024 The Authors. Published by Elsevier B.V.
coredata.prism:publisher: The Authors. Published by Elsevier B.V.
coredata.dc:creator: Ochoa-Pérez, Manuel
Juárez-Gómez, Jorge
Meneses-Aparicio, Sarahy
Guzmán-Hernández, Dafne Sarahia
Lara, Víctor
Rojas-Hernández, Alberto
Ramírez-Silva, María Teresa
coredata.dc:description: This work presents the design and optimization of a new Cr(VI)-selective electrode. The new sensor is based on modifying a glassy carbon electrode with carbon quantum dots, ß-cyclodextrin, and carbon disulfide. The carbon quantum dots aided the electrical properties of the glassy carbon by improving charge transfer; the electropolymerization of ß-cyclodextrin resulted in a polymeric membrane that functions as a recognition element when modified by adding sulfur donor groups to its structure. Modifying the membrane with sulfur atoms gave the sensor excellent selectivity toward Cr(VI) ions. The electrode synthesis was optimized using a 23 factorial design; the factors studied were pH, the number of cycles in the electropolymerization, and the presence or absence of carbon nanoparticles. Eight different electrodes were constructed, and their potentiometric response to different concentrations of Cr(VI) was evaluated for all of them. The analysis of variance of the experimental design found no significant effect of any factor on the response. However, it suggests a strong interaction between the three factors studied. The sensor that presented the best analytical parameters was synthesized at pH 5 and 50 consecutive cycles in the presence of carbon quantum dots. This new electrode, with its response times of 40 s at different concentrations of the metal ion, exhibiting a slope of (66.0 ± 2.1) mV decade−1 and a detection limit of (5.2 ± 0.1)x10−7 mol L−1, can be used at pH between 0 and 3 without the effect of hydronium ions. The proposed electrode has good reproducibility and excellent selectivity against various metal ions and has significant advantages over other analysis methods. Its cost, ease of construction, easy handling, ease of operation, ease of storage and transportation, as well as good performance, short response time, and high selectivity make this electrode a valuable tool for easy, fast, and reliable monitoring of Cr(VI) in water samples, contributing to the safety and health of our environment.
coredata.dcterms:subject: ISE
Cr(VI)
Selectivity
ß-CD
Electropolymerization

Electropolymerization of ß-CD and modification of the membrane

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Successive cyclic voltammogram (50 cycles) obtained in the GCE/ß-CD 6 mmol L−1, in 0.05 mol L−1 phosphate buffer system, pH 5.0, at 100 mV/s scan rate in anodic direction.
Schematic diagram of the modification of GCE with ß-CD and CS2.
X-ray diffractograms for the electrode surface before chromium treatment (a) and after chromium treatment (b).
a) Variation of the potential as a function of time for different additions of Cr(VI), b) Calibration curve of the constructed electrode as a function of Log[Cr(VI)], r2=0.9886.

a) Emission spectra of the CQDs from 330 to 600 nm to an excitation wavelength of 311 nm. b) Successive cyclic voltammograms obtained in the GCE/ CQDs in 0.05 M phosphate buffer system, pH 5.0, at 0.1 Vs−1 scan rate in anodic direction. c) Cyclic voltammograms for perchloric acid solutions (blank) and a 0.33 mM potassium ferrocyanide solution were obtained with the clean GCE and with a GCE modified with CQDs.
pH effect on the potentiometric response of the ISE-ßCD-CS2–8 electrode.
Calibration curve for the ISE-ßCD-CS2–8 electrode.
Potentiometric response of ISE-ßCD-CS2–8 electrode to interfering ions.
The factorial design applied to optimize the electrode.
Electrodes proposed in the literature for the detection of Cr(IV).

ce:legend.ce:simple-para: *The potentiometric constants or concentrations at which the electrode starts to respond to the interfering ion are reported.

head.ce:title: Design and optimization of a Cr(VI)-Selective Electrode based on a polymeric ß-cyclodextrin membrane modified with sulfur donor groups

sa:affiliation.sa:address-line: Av. San Rafael Atlixco #186, Col. Vicentina, D.F., C.P.

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: bDepartamento de Química, CONAHCYT-Universidad Autónoma Metropolitana Iztapalapa, Av. San Rafael Atlixco #186, Col. Vicentina, D.F., C.P., 09340, México

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: This work presents the design and optimization of a new Cr(VI)-selective electrode. The new sensor is based on modifying a glassy carbon electrode with carbon quantum dots, ß-cyclodextrin, and carbon disulfide. The carbon quantum dots aided the electrical properties of the glassy carbon by improving charge transfer; the electropolymerization of ß-cyclodextrin resulted in a polymeric membrane that functions as a recognition element when modified by adding sulfur donor groups to its structure. Modifying the membrane with sulfur atoms gave the sensor excellent selectivity toward Cr(VI) ions. The electrode synthesis was optimized using a 23 factorial design; the factors studied were pH, the number of cycles in the electropolymerization, and the presence or absence of carbon nanoparticles. Eight different electrodes were constructed, and their potentiometric response to different concentrations of Cr(VI) was evaluated for all of them. The analysis of variance of the experimental design found no significant effect of any factor on the response. However, it suggests a strong interaction between the three factors studied. The sensor that presented the best analytical parameters was synthesized at pH 5 and 50 consecutive cycles in the presence of carbon quantum dots. This new electrode, with its response times of 40 s at different concentrations of the metal ion, exhibiting a slope of (66.0 ± 2.1) mV decade−1 and a detection limit of (5.2 ± 0.1)x10−7 mol L−1, can be used at pH between 0 and 3 without the effect of hydronium ions. The proposed electrode has good reproducibility and excellent selectivity against various metal ions and has significant advantages over other analysis methods. Its cost, ease of construction, easy handling, ease of operation, ease of storage and transportation, as well as good performance, short response time, and high selectivity make this electrode a valuable tool for easy, fast, and reliable monitoring of Cr(VI) in water samples, contributing to the safety and health of our environment.

ce:list-item.ce:para: No data was used for the research described in the article.

ce:section.ce:para: Chromium is used in dyes, pigments, leather tanning, wood preservation, and some industries such as textile, oil, and steel manufacturing. The most common forms are Cr(0), Cr(III), and Cr(VI), with Cr(VI) being the most toxic. Disposing of products treated with chromium or containing this metal contaminates the air, water, and soil. The oral, respiratory, or dermal routes absorb it. Various health effects have been attributed to it, specifically on the respiratory, digestive, and reproductive systems, and it is also considered a potent carcinogen [1,2]. According to OSHA (Occupational Safety and Health Administration), the permissible limit for chromium in the air is 0.5 µg/m3, averaged over eight working hours [3]. According to the U.S. Environmental Protection Agency (EPA), the permissible limit for total chromium in drinking water is 0.1 mg/L or 100 ppb [4].

Due to the low detection limits usually reached, the traditional methods for determining Cr(VI) are spectrophotometry, ion exchange chromatography, and atomic absorption spectroscopy, the latter being the most used, where flame atomic absorption spectroscopy stands out. These methods are costly due to their instrumentation and the additional chemical compounds necessary for their execution. Due to this, the scientific community is joining efforts to develop better methods for the quantification of chromium [5,6].

Potentiometric electrodes are another Cr(VI) detection method that has several advantages over traditional methods, such as low manufacturing cost, simplicity, selectivity, short response time, and adequate precision and accuracy. Ion-selective electrodes (ISE) or potentiometric sensors are a subgroup of electrochemical sensors that can be chemosensors modified with a polymer or biosensors modified with chemical and biological materials [7].
The glassy carbon electrode (GCE) can be modified with various compounds and polymers, such as Prussian blue, to synthesize an ISE and detect Cr(IV) in samples with low detection limits [8,9].

Cyclodextrins (CDs) are unique cyclic compounds composed of glucose. They form a lipophilic internal cavity and a hydrophilic surface, making them ideal for interacting with various compounds, such as drugs and ions. Of particular interest are those with 6, 7, and 8 units (α, ß, γ). In ß-CD, the hydroxyl groups can be modified by a diverse functional groups or compounds, enhancing its lipophilicity, and making it a promising candidate for use in ISE membranes [10–12].

Carbon quantum dots (CQDs) have been applied to detect heavy metals in aqueous solutions; however, electrochemistry has yet to be developed in the best way. Despite this, some studies have been reported where CQDs membranes together with an organic compound on a GCE have presented better detections of the analytes and better sensitivity of up to 10 times more than simple GCE [13,14].

In the present work, a new ISE selective to Cr(VI) ions in an aqueous solution is designed and optimized. The electrode is based on modifying a GCE with CQDs and a ß-CD polymeric membrane modified with sulfur donor groups.

All reagents and solutions used were of analytical grade. Deionized water with a resistance of 18 MΩ from a Milli-Q Millipore apparatus was used. 2-hydroxypropyl-ß-cyclodextrin (≥97 %) and CS2 (≥99.9 %) were obtained from Sigma-Aldrich, NaOH (Avantor) and K2HPO4 (Monterey) along with KH2PO4 (J.T Baker) were used to prepare phosphate buffer pH 3 and 5. HClO4 (J.T. Baker) and K2CrO4 (Monterey). Al2O3, TiO2, CH3COONa, BaCO3 and SnCl2 were obtained from J.T. Baker; CaCO3 from Monterey; CuO from Fisher; Pb(NO3)2, Mg(NO3)2, FeCl2, HgCl2 from Merck; CdSO4 from Laitz; (CH3COO)2 Zn, KNO3 from Técnica Química and AgNO3 from Reasol.

The GCE surface modification was carried out in a solution of 2-hydroxypropyl-ß-cyclodextrin (ß-CD) 6.0 mmol L −1 in phosphate buffer 50.0 mmol L−1 pH 5.0. The solution was placed in a three-electrode thermostatted electrochemical cell. A GCE was used as a working electrode, Ag/AgCl (3 M) as a reference, and a platinum wire as an auxiliary electrode. Before synthesizing the sensing membrane, the working electrode was polished in 0.3 µm alumina for 30 min and washed with deionized water and ultrasonic. Then, the electrode was electrochemically cleaned in 0.5 mol L1 sulfuric acid by cyclic voltammetry from −0.75 to 2.0 V at 100 mV/s for 20 cycles using a µAutolab Type III galvanostat potentiostat. For the electropolymerization of ß-CD, the cyclic voltammetry technique was used in a potential window from −2.0 to 2.2 V at 100 mV/s applying 50 cycles with constant stirring [16]. After electropolymerization, the electrode was immersed in a 2.0 mol L1 NaOH solution and stirred for two hours. Finally, 1.0 mL of CS2 was added, and the electrode was immersed in this solution for 17 h, stirring constantly.

For potentiometric measurements, the electrode was conditioned for 10 min in a solution of Cr(VI) 1.0 × 10−5 mol L1 before each experiment. Subsequently, the electrode was conditioned for 20 min before each measurement in a solution of HClO4 0.1 mol L1 for reproducibility and pH tests. The electrode was stored in the HClO4 solution. The potentiometric study was performed against chromium concentrations using a µAutolab Type III galvanostat potentiostat. A two-electrode system was used; the modified electrode was used as a working electrode, and Ag/AgCl (3 M) was used as a reference. These analyses were performed in a conventional electrochemical cell with 10 mL of HClO4 0.1 mol L1. A 1.0 × 10−1 mol L1 Cr(VI) solution was prepared for the potentiometric response, from which successive dilutions were made up to 1.0 × 10−8 mol L1. The effect of pH was determined over a range of 0 - 7 using the dilution of Cr(VI) 1.0 × 10−4 mol L1. The pH was adjusted by diluting HClO4 1.0 mol L1 and NaOH 1.0 mol L1.

The factorial design of 23; three factors, and two levels was performed, leading to the evaluation of the slope and detection limit of the calibration curves. The factors studied in the synthesis of the sensor, including the number of cycles in the electropolymerization of ß-CD (20 and 50 cycles), pH (3.0 and 5.0), and the presence of CQDs (without and with), were found to influence the calibration curves significantly.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Synthesis of CQDs and electrode modification
ce:section.ce:para: The synthesis was done out using a novel approach, as reported in [15]. 5.0 gs of Jamaica flower, a unique source, were weighed into a crucible and charred at 400 °C for 4 h. The ashes were allowed to stand until they reached room temperature. Subsequently, they were ground to fine powder and added in 30 mL of deionized water, forming a black mixture centrifuged for 30 min at 4000 RPM. Finally, it was filtered and preserved in an amber bottle and were then characterized by fluorescence spectroscopy on a PerkinElmer LS 55 fluorometer. From the obtained solution of CQDs, 1 mL was taken and added to a 10 mL phosphate buffer pH 5.0; the resulting solution was used for treatment. The electrode was treated with the CQDs by cyclic voltammetry in a potential range of 2.2 to −2.5 V at 100 mV/s applying ten successive cycles. The electrode was characterized by x-ray with a Siemens D500 diffractometer with θ2θ geometry and Match 3 database.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Electropolymerization of ß-CD and modification of the membrane
ce:section.ce:para: The successive cyclic voltammograms of ß-CD on the GCE vs Ag/AgCl surface are shown in Fig. 1 . In the first cycle, a cathodic peak (1) occurs at - 0.58 V. The second cycle present two anodic peaks are formed at 0.6 V (2) and 1.5 V (3). From cycle 27, another cathodic peak (4) starts to form at - 0.8 V. These current peaks grow successively with increasing cycles, indicating that a ß-CD membrane is forming on the surface of GCE. According to experimental studies, the first current peak formed is related to the success of the synthesis; the higher and more defined this peak is, the more likely the electropolymerization will succeed. The first cycle presents a voltammetric behavior different from the others; from this cycle, the monomer starts to oxidize on the surface of the GCE, and electropolymerization occurs in the following cycles [16]. Finally, the GCE was obtained and modified with ß-CD.

The ß-CD membrane was chemically modified by adding sulfur donor groups to enhance the selectivity of the electrode. The ß-CD modified GCE was immersed in an aqueous solution of NaOH 2.0 mol L1 for two hours with constant stirring, then 1.0 mL of CS2 was added, and the reaction was allowed to proceed for 17 h with constant stirring. The excess hydroxide was removed from the electrode by rinsing it with plenty of deionized water, and this sensor was used in the potentiometric experiments against different chromium concentrations. A schematic of the GCE modification is shown in Fig. 2 .

The formation of the ß-CD film is performed by electropolymerization due to the interaction of the active center of the ß-CD formed in the oxidation with the electrode surface, joining it using covalent bonds, and the monomers are successively coupled to form the polymer. After this step, the reaction with NaOH works to deprotonate the hydroxyl groups available in the polymer, so when reacting with CS2, the oxygen with free electrons of the polymer binds to the molecule's carbon. The reactions on the electrode surface for the screening tests in HClO4 solution after Cr(IV) additions are also depicted in Fig. 2. The sulfurs added to the membrane interact with the Cr(IV) ions in the solution, forming a stable complex. In addition, the Cr(IV) ions most likely also interact with the ClO4 − ions present in the solution to form a complex octahedral structure. X-ray diffractograms for the electrode surface before chromium treatment (a) and after chromium treatment (b) are shown in Fig. 3 . The X-ray characterization showed the presence of an organic polymer with sulfur atoms in its structure. In the case of electrode with chromium, the study revealed the presence of hydrated chromium compounds with sulfates and even chromium compounds with carbonyl groups. These results fully support the hypothesis of the model described in Fig. 2.

The potentiometric response of the modified electrode was evaluated against different Cr(VI) concentrations in a conventional electrochemical cell with 10 mL of HClO4 0.1 mol L1 and using Ag/AgCl as a reference. Fig. 4a shows the electrode potentiometric response as a function of time for different Cr(IV) concentrations. It can be observed that the electrode potential remains constant until a new aliquot of the metal is added to the system, and then the potential increases drastically until it remains constant again. The response time of the electrode is approximately 40 s. In Fig. 4b, the plot of E vs Log[Cr(VI)] is reported, and it can be seen that the electrode potential increases linearly with the logarithm of the chromium concentration, exhibiting a super Nernstian slope of (130.0 ± 8.1) mV decade−1. These experimental results support the hypothesis that modifying the ß-CD membrane with CS2 Cr(VI) could coordinate with electron donor groups. Therefore, the constructed sensor may work to detect Cr(VI) in aqueous solution.

Carbon quantum dots (CQDs) were synthesized and added to the sensor to improve its analytical performance parameters. Fig. 5a presents the emission spectrum of the synthesized carbon nanoparticles in an aqueous solution; the nanoparticles present a maximum emission of around 411 nm when excited with a wavelength of 311 nm. The asymmetric emission spectrum is typical of CQDs. Therefore, the synthesis has been successful [29,30]. On the other hand, consecutive cyclic voltammograms for 500 µL of CQDs solution added to 10 mL of 0.05 mol L1 phosphate buffer and pH 5.0 are presented in Fig. 5b. An anodic peak near 0.6 V and a cathodic peak around −0.6 V are observed, probably related to the oxidation and reduction of the carboxylic groups present in the structure of this type of materials. After the fifth cycle, a second anodic peak is observed at 1.5 V, possibly associated with the oxidation of the products being formed on the electrode surface. The peak current increases as the cycles increase, suggesting that a CQD film forms on the electrode surface, which could follow the exact mechanism of the ß-CD membrane. The electrical properties of the CQDs were verified by cyclic voltammetry. Fig. 5c shows the voltammetric response of the GCE unmodified for a perchloric acid solution (black line) where there is no evidence of any redox process; the green line is the voltammetric response of the GCE unmodified for a solution of potassium ferrocyanide 0. 33 mM in perchloric acid, in this case, the typical redox processes of the ferricyanide/ferrocyanide system can be appreciated; in red, the voltammetric response of the GCE modified with the CQDs nanoparticles for the same 0.33 mM potassium ferrocyanide solution in perchloric acid is shown, where the typical redox processes are observed but with significantly important differences. The electrode modified with CQDs presents higher capacitance, in addition, the peak currents of the redox processes increase significantly when the electrode is modified with the nanoparticles, these results demonstrate that the CQDs improve the electrical properties of the GCE.

The electrode response was optimized using a 23 factorial design (three factors, two levels). The factors studied were the number of cycles in the electropolymerization of ß-CD, the pH of the medium, and the presence of CQDs, with levels of 20 and 50 cycles, presence, and absence of CQDs, and 3.0 or 5.0 of pH. With this experimental design, eight different electrodes were constructed, and Table 1 presents the results obtained, where the slope, limit of detection (LOD), and linear interval (LOL) of the potentiometric responses of each electrode were analyzed as a function of the logarithm of the Cr(VI) concentration. The slope values for all electrodes were found to be greater than predicted by the Nernst equation for a divalent anion; these results are not necessarily discouraging since it is known that many potentiometric electrodes do not follow such behavior. The LOD range is 10−4 to 10−6 mol L1, and the LOL range is 10−6 to 10−2 for all electrodes. On examining the values obtained for these factors and the individual calibration curves for each electrode, it was determined that the sensor synthesized with 50 cycles at pH 5.0 and with the presence of CQDs (ISE-ßCD-CS2–8) presented the best analytical parameters. For this reason, it was decided to use this electrode as the working electrode in the subsequent experiments applied in this study. The analysis of variance suggests that none of the individual factors significantly affect the slope value. However, the interaction between the three factors significantly affects this parameter. The analysis of variance showed normal behavior in the experimental data and homoscedasticity among the variances.

Fig. 6 shows the graph of the potential of the ISE-ßCD-CS2–8 electrode as a function of the medium's pH. It can be seen that from pH 0 to pH 3.0, the potential remains relatively constant; after pH 3.0, the potential decreases drastically as the pH increases. The experimental results show a strong interaction of the membrane with the hydronium ions; therefore, to avoid this effect, the pH where the Cr(VI) concentration is measured should be kept below 3.0.

Three electrodes were constructed on different days and with the same configuration of the ISE-ßCD-CS2–8 sensor to study the reproducibility of the potentiometric results. Different Cr(VI) aliquots were added to 10 mL of a 0.1 mol L1 HClO4 solution. The average potentiometric response of the electrodes is shown in Fig. 7 ; these results show an average slope of (66.2 ± 2.0) mV decade−1 over a concentration range of (1.0 × 10−7 to 1.0 × 10−2) mol L1. The results demonstrate that the ISE-ßCD-CS2–8 electrode exhibits high reproducibility.

Their selectivity characterizes the ISE to the ion they are made, so it was essential to evaluate the electrode's response to possible interfering ions. The interference of differently charged metal cations such as Al(III), Cu(II), Ca(II), Pb(II), Ag(I), Ba(II), K(I), Mg(II), Na(I), Zn(II), Cd(II), Fe(II), Hg(II), Sn(II), Ti(II), Cr(III) was evaluated using the separated solutions method [17-18]. The selectivity coefficients were calculated with the utmost scientific rigor, as recommended by IUPAC, using the semiempirical Nikolsky-Eisenman Eq. (1), a widely accepted and reliable method in our field.
(1)
E
I S E
=
E
0
+
R T
z i
F
ln
[
∑
a i
+
K
i , j
P o t
a j
z i
z j
]

Here, EISE represents the electrode potential, E0 is the standard potential, ai and aj denote the activity of the primary ion (in this case, Cr(VI)), and the interfering ion, respectively. zi and zj signify the charges of the primary ion and the interfering ion. The activities of the ions can be equated to the molar concentration using the dilute solution approximation. By equating the potentials in the primary and interfering ion calibration curves, we derive Eq. (2) for the potentiometric selectivity constants, which is a key component of our research.
(2)
K
i / j
p o t
=
[
M i
+
z i
]
[
M j
+
z j
]
z i
/
z j

However, when using this equation, there were some drawbacks; because the quotient of the charges (zi/zj ) is significant due to Cr(VI), the result of the selectivity coefficients results in irrational values. Fig. 8 shows the potentiometric responses of the ISE-ßCD-CS2–8 electrode against the different metal ions compared with the primary ion. The only ions that interfere with the response of the primary ion are Hg(II) and Ag(I). On the one hand, mercury presents a slope of 47.9 mV decade−1 but at concentrations higher than 10−4 mol L1, while silver presents a slope of 156.8 mV decade−1 from concentrations of 10−3 mol L1. Compared to the LOD obtained for Cr(VI), which was (5.2 ± 0.1)x10−7 mol L − 1, the interference of these ions occurs at much higher concentrations. Therefore, the ISE-ßCD-CS2–8 sensor exhibits excellent selectivity at 10−7 and 10−4 mol L concentrations.

Table 2 presents some ISE and other electrode types proposed in the literature for Cr(IV) detection. The LOD for the electrode proposed in this work is comparable with those reported in the literature; the linear range is, in some cases, higher than those reported for other sensors. The ISE proposed here stands out in its high selectivity since it does not present interference in the concentration range of 10−7 and 10−4 mol L1. The sensor proposed in this work presents advantages over those reported in the literature. From the point of view of its fabrication, it is easy to build since the GCE is easily modified with the CQDs and the ß-CD membrane. Even incorporating carbon nanoparticles is simple and cheap using hibiscus flowers, without leaving aside that it is an environmentally friendly synthesis. All these points lead to the conclusion that the new electrode proposed here is a good alternative for detecting Cr(VI) in aqueous solutions compared to the prominent analysis methods.

A new Cr(VI)-selective electrode with excellent response was constructed and optimized. The new sensor is based on the electrochemical modification of a GCE with carbon nanoparticles, ß-CD, and sulfur donor groups. Analysis of variance suggests that the pH, number of cycles, and presence or absence of CQDs do not significantly affect the electrode response. However, the ANOVA exhibits a strong interaction between the three factors studied for the synthesis. The electrode presents response times of 40 s, which makes it possible to use it in continuous flow analysis. The electrode presented an excellent response towards Cr(VI) ions from concentrations of 10−7 and up to 10−2 mol L−1, exhibiting a super Nernstian slope of (66.0 ± 2.1) mV per decade of concentration and making possible its use in a pH range of (0 - 3) without the influence of the concentration of hydronium ions. The main advantage of the proposed sensor is that the sensing membrane does not contain additional additives such as plasticizers or ionic carriers; by modifying the ß-CD polymer with sulfur donor groups, the membrane itself is transformed into the Cr(VI)-selective ionophore, thus reducing the manufacturing time by not requiring the synthesis of the ionophore separately and reducing costs. Finally, the proposed electrode presented an excellent selectivity against various metal ions and remarkable advantages over other analysis methods. Its cost, ease of construction, easy handling, ease of operation, ease of storage and transportation, good performance, short response time, and high selectivity make this electrode a good option for easy, fast, and reliable monitoring of Cr(VI) in water samples.

Manuel Ochoa-Pérez: Writing – original draft, Visualization, Investigation. Jorge Juárez-Gómez: Writing – review & editing, Writing – original draft, Visualization, Validation, Methodology, Investigation, Funding acquisition, Formal analysis, Data curation, Conceptualization. Sarahy Meneses-Aparicio: Investigation. Dafne Sarahia Guzmán-Hernández: Writing – review & editing, Investigation. Víctor Lara: Writing – review & editing, Methodology. Alberto Rojas-Hernández: Writing – review & editing. María Teresa Ramírez-Silva: Writing – review & editing.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: MOP thanks Conahcyt for the grant (1278466) to complete his master's studies. JJG, DSGH, ARH and MTRS thank SNII for the financial support they received.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Plomo, cromo III y cromo VI y sus efectos sobre la salud humana

ce:other-ref.ce:textref: Agency for Toxic Substance and Disease Registry, Cromo. https://www.atsdr.cdc.gov/es/phs/es_phs7.html, 2012.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Sensors for detection of Cr(VI) in water: a review
